text:
s
s  The reaction-diffusion PDE is widely employed across
e
various пђБelds,  including biology [2],  chemistry [10],  and
e
[ epidemiology [11] to model phenomena such as heat dis-
 
 
tribution,  chemical  reactions,  and the spread of diseases.
1
Due to its broad applicability,  the reaction-diffusion PDE
v
4 has been extensively studied,  in particular,  in  terms of
2 stabilization under digital  implementation effects,  see,  for
9 example, [6], [14], [16], [21], [25], [26], [28] and references
5
therein.  In digital  implementations of  feedback laws  for
1
.  reaction-diffusion PDEs,  the resulting closed-loop systems
1 may be subject  to input delay and quantization. Consider,
0
for example, the case of epidemics spreading described by
5
a reaction diffusion PDE system [11], which may be subject
2
:  to delay, due to, e.g.,  testing or measures imposition lags
v
i  [24], [32] and quantization, due to, e.g., discrete quarantine
measures imposition (as implementation of measures chosen
X
r  from a continuum are practically infeasible). The presence
a
of input delay and quantization may deteriorate performance
or even destabilize the closed-loop system.
For  this  reason, besides  results on control of  systems
under quantization, see, for instance, [3], [4], [23] that ex-
amine linear systems with quantized measurements and [22]
that addresses general nonlinear systems under quantization,
there are results dealing with the effect of quantization in
delay systems.  In particular,  [8]  focuses on linear  time-
delay systems with saturation,  [9]  investigates logarithmic

Florent Koudohode (fkoudohode@tuc.gr) and Nikolaos Bekiaris-Liberis
(bekiaris-liberis@ece.tuc.gr) are with Department of Electrical and
Computer Engineering, Technical University of Crete, 73100, Greece.

n-diffusion PDE with input delay
nput quantization
ikolaos Bekiaris-Liberis
quantizers within an event-based control framework, and [5]
considers nonlinear systems with state delay and quantiza-
tion. Stabilization of various classes of PDEs under static
quantization has also been addressed. In particular, [7] and

B. Properties of the Quantizer
The state u of  the plant and the actuator state v are
available only in quantized form. We consider here dynamic
quantizers with an adjustable parameter of  the form (see,
e.g., [3], [18], [22])
u  v
q¬µ(u, v) = (q1¬µ(u), q2¬µ(v)) = ¬µq1  , ¬µq2  ,

(34)

вЖТ  R  R

properties
P1: If  (u, v)  M, then  (q1(u)  u, q2(v)  v)  вИЖ,

for some positive constants M,M, and вИЖ, with M > вИЖ and
MЋЖ < M.
2
Considering the L  norm of the reaction-diffusion PDE
вИТ
state requires to deпђБne the properties of the quantizer ac-
2
cordingly in L -norm, as well as to impose the additional
2
assumption that  the quantizer function also belongs to L
(that is tacitly assumed in the deпђБnition of q1). An example of
a quantizer that satisпђБes this property is when the quantizer
may be assumed to also satisfy a speciпђБc sector condition
(such as,  e.g.,  the logarithmic quantizer [9]).

In order  to
actually implement a quantizer using typical quantizer func-
tions (see, e.g., [22]), when the quantizer is deпђБned using
2
an L  norm, one would have to also use, e.g., a density
вИТ  2
argument, to guarantee the approximation of its L  norm by
вИТ
the Euclidean norm of a пђБnite-dimensional counterpart. The
work in [30] is relevant here, as it introduces and implements
quantizers in different norms. We also note that we choose
to deпђБne the properties of the quantizer in terms of the norm
of the complete inпђБnite-dimensional state of the PDE-PDE
system, as the system considered is equipped with this norm,
in correspondence with respective deпђБnitions in the пђБnite-
dimensional case, see, e.g., [22].
We further notice that we consider a case in which the
measured transport PDE state is also subject to quantization.
If  the transport PDE state corresponds to a delayed state
and if  the delayed state is available without quantization,
1  q  u

one could employ the past  input values in the predictor-
feedback law. Moreover, as  in [18], we assume a single
tunable parameter ¬µ for simplicity in control design and
analysis, which is also practically reasonable in scenarios
where, for example, a single computer with a single camera
is used for measurements collection.

In this paper, we achieved the global asymptotic stability
of an unstable reaction-diffusion PDE with input delay under
both state and input quantization, developing a switched
predictor-feedback control  law and combining the back-
stepping method with small-gain and input-to-state stability
techniques. Future research could explore designing event-
triggered controllers under quantization,  inspired by [19].
The challenges for such a design would be the construction
of the event-triggering mechanism such that it depends only
on quantized measurements, as well as the proof of Zeno
phenomenon avoidance, due to the non-differentiability of
the quantizer function.
